coredata.dc:title: Picomolar reversible Hg(II) solid-state sensor based on carbon dots in double heterostructure colloidal photonic crystals
coredata.dc:creator: Eftekhari, Ehsan
Wang, Wentai
Li, Xiang
A, Nikhil
Wu, Zhiqing
Klein, Robin
Cole, Ivan S.
Li, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Mercury contamination in water is a persistent issue due to both natural geological and anthropogenic activities. Portable, facile and affordable sensors for detection and sensing different species of mercuries are highly desirable. We report a highly effective fluorescent, solid state sensor with high sensitivity, good selectivity and excellent reversibility for Hg(II) ion. Hg(II)-responsive carbon dots immobilised polystyrene spheres were fabricated as a middle layer in double heterostructure colloidal photonic crystal film. Significant fluorescence enhancement was achieved due to doubly resonant of the modes of photonic crystals and multi beam interface inside the double heterostructure film. The amplified fluorescence enhances the sensitivity of detection, achieving a detection limit of 91pM for Hg(II) ion, even 17 times lower than that of carbon dots solution probe. The polystyrene-based film sensor is negligibly responsive to other metal ions and can easily be recovered by rinsing with cysteine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Double heterostructure
Photonic crystals
Carbon dots
Sensing
Mercury sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and stability of CDs@F spheres
Hg(II) ion detection sensitivity and selectivity
Reversibility of the double heterostructure film sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of fabrication of double heterostructure E-(CDs@F)-E and multiple-beam interference effect in the CDs@F middle layer. Top: Illustration of CDs immobilised on the surface of F spheres in the middle layer.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@F after treating with ethanol, acetone and THF using various DMF vol.%. (b) The size distribution of F spheres in different composition of DMF measured by DLS. Inset: The fluorescence spectra of supernatant solutions of treated CDs@F and CDs-F samples by water, ethanol, acetone and THF.

(a) Normalized FL excitation and emission spectrum of CDs and normalized transmission spectra of double heterostructure E-(CDs@F)-E. (b) The fluorescence spectra double heterostructure E-(CDs@F)-E, heterostructure E-(CDs@F) and monolithic CDs@F film compare to control film. Inset: FL enhancement factor for all samples, measured as the fluorescence intensity ratio between the samples and the control.

(a) The FL response of double heterostructure E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor with various concentrations of Hg(II). Inset: stern-volmer plot concentration-dependent FL linear response of the E-(CDs@F)-E by Hg(II). (b) FL spectra of E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor in wastewater with different concentrations of Hg(II). Inset: The relationship between (F0/F)-1 and Hg(II) concentrations within the range of 0–200nM (c) Selectivity of the detection of Hg(II) ion by the double heterostructure E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor over other metal ions. The concentration of all metal ions was 100μM. (d) The FL response of E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor with 1μM concentrations of Hg(II), 100μM concentrations of Fe(III) and blank water with and without of SHPP with concentrations of 1mM.

(a) Illustration of detecting Hg(II) ions by E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor. After reaction with Hg(II) ions, NH2–Hg(II)–NH2 complexes can be formed which results in fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence of the sensor can be recovered by removing the Hg(II) ions using cysteine, therefore, the sensor can be regenerated and reused. (b) Reversible changes of the FL intensities at wavelength of 360nm of the E-(CD@F)-E film sensor when immersed alternately in the solutions of 10nM Hg(II) ions and 1.0mM cysteine for six regeneration cycles.

head.ce:title: Picomolar reversible Hg(II) solid-state sensor based on carbon dots in double heterostructure colloidal photonic crystals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Queensland Micro- and Nanotechnology Centre & School of Engineering, Griffith University, Nathan, QLD 4111, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Queensland Micro- and Nanotechnology Centre & School of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIRO Materials Science and Engineering, Clayton, VIC 3168, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Queensland Micro– and Nanotechnology Centre & School of Engineering, Griffith University, Nathan, QLD 4111, Australia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury contamination in water is a persistent issue due to both natural geological and anthropogenic activities. Portable, facile and affordable sensors for detection and sensing different species of mercuries are highly desirable. We report a highly effective fluorescent, solid state sensor with high sensitivity, good selectivity and excellent reversibility for Hg(II) ion. Hg(II)-responsive carbon dots immobilised polystyrene spheres were fabricated as a middle layer in double heterostructure colloidal photonic crystal film. Significant fluorescence enhancement was achieved due to doubly resonant of the modes of photonic crystals and multi beam interface inside the double heterostructure film. The amplified fluorescence enhances the sensitivity of detection, achieving a detection limit of 91pM for Hg(II) ion, even 17 times lower than that of carbon dots solution probe. The polystyrene-based film sensor is negligibly responsive to other metal ions and can easily be recovered by rinsing with cysteine.

ce:section.ce:para: Amongst all heavy metal contaminants in water, mercury is listed on top in terms of the spreading scale and toxic potency [1]. Due to its low boiling point, the emission of mercury is almost inevitable, from both anthropogenic and natural activities, such as volcanos, forest fires, coal combustion and mining activities. Because Hg(0) can be easily oxidized to water soluble Hg(II), mercury contamination of water and soil is bound to happen. To mediate its environmental impact, it is highly important to be able to continuously and accurately monitor the mercury level in water.

Many analytical methods for Hg(II) detection have been developed including quartz crystal microbalance [2], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) technique [3], surface plasmon resonances [4], fluorescence chemosensors [5] and electrochemical methods [6], etc. Among them, fluorescence (FL) chemosensors have received increasing attention in recent years, because of their compatibility with portable devices and reduced cost. [7] Recently, carbon nanodots [8], a class of carbon-based nanoparticles that comprise discrete carbogenic nanoparticles with sizes below 10nm and highly photoluminescent, have been shown to exhibit excellent sensitivity and selectivity to Hg(II) as a fluorescence probe. Li and co-workers [9] have reported an organosilane functionalized carbon dots (CDs) with nanomolar sensitivity (detection limit: 1.35nM), high stability in electrolytes and organic solvents.

However, most research and development of the fluorescence-based sensors remain at solution based set-up, i.e. the sensing molecules or nanoparticles need to be dispersed in solutions for functioning as a sensor. This may be caused by reduced or even loss of sensitivity when sensing agents are immobilized on a solid surface. Solid-state sensors are highly desirable because they are easier to handle and transport, more compatible with small-footprint devices, and more stable. Very importantly, solid-state sensors can allow washing steps, hence constituting a sensor, which, by strict definition, should respond to its analytes reversibly.

In selecting a solid-state matrix for immobilizing the fluorescent sensing agents, colloidal photonic crystals (CPhCs) have recently received keen attention because of their extraordinary enhancement effect on signal intensity and the ease in fabrication [10–13]. Photonic crystals can strongly modulate electromagnetic waves and, with sufficient dielectric contrast and an appropriate geometry, may exhibit a photonic band gap (PBG), which affect photons in a manner similar to the effect of semiconductor energy band gaps on electrons and diffract the light with the wavelength located into their PBGs. The CPhCs films could play the role as a Bragg mirror to enhance the intensity of excitation light and/or fluorescence with the wavelength located into their PBGs and thus improve the sensitivity of the sensor [14,15]. Moreover, by combing photonic modes with other optical effects, such as plasmonics [16] and multiple beam interference [17], the fluorescence signal can be further amplified by several-fold. The strategy of combing with multiple beam interference results in an all-polymer film, therefore significantly more affordable as a technology solution. One of the technical challenges in employing CPhCs as the substrate is how to stably incorporate the fluorescent sensing molecules or nanoparticles. Eftekhari et al. [18] have demonstrated an ‘under the skin’ coating method can stably anchor Rhodamine B in CPhC polymer film with uncompromised fluorescence enhancement effect.

In this work, we have adopted a novel CPhC superstructure comprising three layers of monolithic CPhCs [19]. A double heterostructure CPhC is formed by successive deposition of CPhCs in a sequence of E-F-E from bottom to top (where E refers to the monolithic CPhC with a periodicity overlapping the excitation wavelength and F refers to the monolithic CPhC with a periodicity overlapping the emission wavelength). The thickness of the F layer is tuned to support constructive multiple beam interference of the excitation wavelength. Hg(II)-responsive CDs were immobilized by ‘under the skin coating’ method [18] on the surface of F polystyrene spheres (CDs@F), which were deposited as a middle layer in double heterostructure (E-(CDs@F)-E). In such a double heterostructure, the two E layers both reflect any excitation light that may propagate in the (CDs@F) layer. Hence, when the thickness of (CDs@F) supports constructive interference, the excitation light is effectively recycled many times (photon recycling) through the F layer, increasing the excitation efficiency [19]. Therefore, it would be interesting to explore the extent of sensitivity enhancement that the double heterostructure could afford to the fluorescent sensing agent.

This study is comprised of four parts: 1) Development of an effective immobilization method for incorporating CDs on polystyrene surface without compromising their sensitivity to analytes. 2) Investigation of E-(CDs@F)-E fluorescence enhancement. 3) Evaluation of E-(CDs@F)-E film’s sensitivity and selectivity to Hg(II) in various water samples. 4) Testing the sensor reversibility of E-(CDs@F)-E film. Owing to the novel immobilising approach and double heterostructure, the FL intensity significantly enhanced and the detection limit was improved to 91 pM for Hg(II), which is significantly lower than previously reported [20–25]. Moreover, high level of sensing reversibility has also been achieved by this film sensor. Such significant sensitivity, selectivity and reversibility of E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor shows the potential for further solid-based sensor development in complex water samples and harsh environments.

Dimethyl formamide (DMF), Tetrahydrofuran (THF), acrylic acid (AA), acetone and ethanol (all purchased from Sigma Aldrich), were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. N-(b-aminoethyl)-c-aminopropylmethyl- dimethoxysilane (AEAPMS) was purchased from Beijing Shenda Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Glass slides were used as the substrates for fabrication of CPhCs.

The low temperature (150°C) solvothermal synthesis of organosilane-functionalized CDs with dual long chain functional groups of −NH2 and −Si(OCH3)3 uses citric acid as the carbon source and AEAPMS as the solvent, which has been detailed in our previous study [9]. The as-prepared CDs possess abundant amine groups on surface and show excellent sensitivity and selectivity to Hg(II) [9].

The size selection of polystyrene spheres (PS) was based on the excitation and emission peak of CDs using Bragg’s diffraction calculation [26].
(1)
λ max
= 2
d
( 111 )
n
e f f
2
−
sin 2
θ
= 2
2 3
⋅ D ⋅
n
e f f
2
−
sin 2
θ
Where λ max indicates the wavelength value of the reflection peak, d(111) the distance between adjacent (111) lattice planes, D the sphere diameter and neff is the effective refractive index of the structure. Therefore, Acrylic acid functionalised PS spheres with the two different diameter sizes of 185±5nm (E) and 230±5 (F) nm with carboxyl group on the surface were synthesized by an emulsifier free emulsion polymerisation method and purified by several cycles of centrifugation as per earlier reports [27].

To experimentally investigate the difference between introducing the CDs on the F spheres, two approaches were used in this study. 1) Superficial coating: F polystyrene suspension and MilliQ water (5:5vol%) were first purified by several cycles of centrifugation. F spheres were functionalized with acrylic acid to endow abundant amount of –COOH functional groups on the surface of polystyrene spheres, which could have strong electrostatic attraction with the NH2 groups on CDs. Then, 2mL of washed 230nm F suspension was mixed with 2mL of CDs (10−3 molL−1) for 24h. Finally, CDs coated on surface of F spheres were washed and purified by several cycles of centrifugation to remove excess CDs. This sample donated as CDs-F spheres. 2) Under-the-skin coating: CDs immobilised on F spheres (CDs@F) were obtained by a diffusion and entrapment method [28]. In detail, 2mL of CDs (10−3 molL−1) dissolved in DMF was added drop-wise to 2mL aqueous suspension of F spheres (5vol%). After a period of mixing ranging from 2min up to 15min, the CDs coated F spheres were washed and purified by several cycles of centrifugation.

To investigate the fluorescence enhancement, three films were fabricated on the glass substrate by horizontal deposition method [18]: 1) Monolithic CDs@F film was fabricated by self-assembly of CDs@F spheres. 2) Heterostructure of E-(CDs@F) film was fabricated by horizontal deposition of CDs@F spheres on top of the monolithic E layer. 3) Double heterostructure E-(CDs@F)-E was fabricated by sequential horizontal deposition method [19]. The glass substrates were first treated with a Piranha solution to ensure super hydrophilic surfaces.

To illustrate the difference in selecting the reference point for FL enhancement factor calculation, we have prepared a control sample. F spheres were self-assembled by horizontal method on the glass then heated at 120°C for 2h to melt and form an amorphous film. 2mL of CDs (10−3 molL−1) was then infiltrated into the amorphous film. This reference sample is referred to as a control film. In this study, we fabricated monolithic, heterostructure and double heterostructure films with same thickness of 6μm.

The four step solvent treatments were designed for stability test as follow: 1) CDs@F and CDs-F were separately mixed in DI water by ultrasonication for 5 mins. Centrifugation was then applied to separate the spheres and the solution, and the supernatant solution was collected for FL analysis. 2) CDs@F and CDs-F were separately mixed in 98% ethanol by ultrasonication for 5 mins. Centrifugation was then applied to separate the spheres and the solution, and the supernatant solution was collected for FL analysis. 3) The ethanol treated CDs@F and CDs-F spheres were separately mixed in 98% acetone by ultrasonication for 5min. Centrifugation was then applied to separate the spheres and the solution, and the supernatant solution was collected for FL analysis. 4) The ethanol/acetone treated CDs@F and CDs-F spheres were separately dispersed in 50% THF by ultrasonication for 5min, respectively. Supernatant solution and the spheres were separated by centrifugation. The supernatant from each step was checked by FL measurement to measure of leached CDs from the coated spheres.

Secondary wastewater sample was collected from Redland Wastewater Treatment Plant, Queensland, Australia. Wastewater sample was filtered with a 0.22μm syringe filter firstly to remove large particles. 2mL of filtrated water solution was transferred to a quartz cuvette for FL measurement. Hg(II) solution was then added into the vial step-wisely to increase the concentration from 100pM. Each time after adding Hg(II) solution, a 15min time interval was given to allow a good diffusion-driven mixing in the vial, before the FL measurement.

Detection of Hg(II) in pure water was performed at room temperature. Double heterostructure of E-(CDs@F)-E were immersed in pure water, and solutions containing Hg(II), Fe(III) as well as various metal ions such as: Na+, Ag+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Cr3+each at a concentration of 100μM. The selectivity of CDs also evaluated against K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, respectively, at a concentration of 1000ppm. The sensing procedure consists of a) E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor was immersed in pure water into the quartz cuvette as blank sample. b) Hg(II) solutions were then added into the cuvette step-wise to increase the concentration. Each time after adding Hg(II) solution, a 15min time interval was given to allow a good diffusion-driven mixing in the cuvette, before the FL measurement. The results demonstrated that there were different decreases of the FL intensity for the solution of the Hg(II) ion compared to blank water.

The surface morphology of E-(CDs@F)-E was examined by scanning electron microscope (SEM) using a JEOL 7001, operated at 15kV. Transmission spectra were measured on Agilent 8453 UV–vis spectrometer. The hydrodynamic particle size was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Malvern Instrument Zetasizer Nano-ZS. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on Thermo Scientific Lumina fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and stability of CDs@F spheres
ce:section.ce:para: For stably incorporating the CDs on PS sphere surface, under-the-skin method is more effective. In order to avoid excessive swelling and remarkable change of the PS surface morphologies by DMF, the DMF composition for ideal CDs integration was examined by varying volume percentage (vol.%) with constant amount of F spheres and CDs. Fig. 1 (a) shows the FL intensity of supernatant solution of CDs@F after treating first with ethanol then acetone and finally THF with different DMF compositions. Using 30vol.% DMF resulted in no FL signal from supernatant solutions after treating with all three solvents. Indicating an optimum amount of the CDs immobilised on the surface of F spheres at 30vol.% DMF compared to lower and higher composition. This is likely the optimum DMF concentration which created the suitable pore size on F sphere size and allowed a right amount of CDs to penetrate into F spheres. Higher concentration of DMF probably created larger pores, which allowed CDs to diffuse in as well as diffuse out at a similar level.

We also examined the influence of DMF on F spheres by measuring the size of spheres with different DMF compositions by DLS. Fig. 1(b) shows the DLS result for the effect of 10–60% DMF on the diameter of F sphere; the 10% DMF treatment had the least effect on polystyrene size, almost remaining at their original size of 230nm. However, the FL intensity of 10% DMF treated spheres shows that swelling of PS spheres was not enough for CDs to penetrating into the PS matrix. By using 30% vol of DMF, original size of F spheres slightly changed to 235nm but swelled enough for CDs to penetrate into PS matrix for highest FL intensity.

In order to examine how robust the CDs are attached on the surface of F spheres in water or solvents, we designed the stability test to compare the under-the-skin coating approach with electrostatic interaction-facilitated approach. Inset Fig. 1(b) shows the FL intensity of supernatant solution (or washing solution) of CDs@F and CDs-F after treating with water, ethanol, acetone and THF. In all four washing steps, the washing solutions for CDs-F all resulted in yellow-coloured supernatants, indicating that some of the CDs leached out from the F spheres. In contrast, there were no detectable fluorescence signals from all four washing solutions of CDs@F, suggesting that CDs was strongly entrapped on the surface of CDs@F. The results confirm that the diffusion and penetration approach is very effective in immobilising CDs molecules on the surface of PS spheres.

In order to obtain the most sensitive film sensor, we fabricated the double heterostructure film sensor. We have designed E-(CDs@F)-E double heterostructure film sensor as follows: E-(CDs@F)-E is formed in a sequence of layers from bottom to top, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The E layer lattice constant is chosen to create a partial bandgap at excitation wavelength of the CDs, whereas the CDs@F layer lattice constant is chosen for a partial bandgap at the emission wavelength. The key novelty in such a double heterostructure is that the two E layers both reflect any excitation light that may propagate in the CDs@F layer. Hence, the bandgap of CDs@F layer itself overlapped the CDs emission wavelength, and can therefore enhance FL intensity in its own right through coherent scattering.

We synthesised PS spheres of 185±5nm for E layers and 230±5nm for CDs@F layer to match the excitation wavelength (360nm) and emission wavelength (460nm) maxima of CDs, respectively. Fig. S1 presents SEM image of CDs@F spheres and E-(CDs@F)-E film fabricated on glass, where large-area, high quality colloidal crystal structures are revealed, showing the effectiveness of the simple horizontal method in obtaining uniform crystals and congruent interfaces. E-(CDs@F)-E film exhibit face-cantered cubic (FCC) crystal morphology with (1 1 1) planes oriented parallel to the surface of the substrate.

The influence of the PBG on the FL intensity of E-(CDs@F)-E film is demonstrated in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a) overlays the excitation and emission peaks of CDs, and the two stopbands of E-(CDs@F)-E film. It can be observed that the CDs excitation peak (at 360nm), i.e., strongest absorption peak is located at the blue edge of the stopband that belongs to the E layers, and that the CDs emission peak (at 460nm) also overlaps the blue edge of the stopband that is caused by the middle F layer [14,19].

Fig. 2(b) shows the FL spectra of incorporated CDs in E-(CDs@F)-E double heterostructure, in comparison to CDs@F monolithic, E-(CDs@F) heterostructure and the control films. It is obvious that all three CPhC films have augmented the fluorescence intensity of CDs significantly compared to the control film. It is also apparent that the variation of CPhC structures has a profound impact on the magnitude of enhancement. The monolithic CDs@F film, whose lattice constant is matched with the emission wavelength, yielded a very effective fluorescence intensity enhancement with an enhancement factor of 33-fold, due to coherent scattering of emission light. The fluorescence enhancement by heterostructure of E-(CDs@F) is stronger, owing to the enhanced light concentration of excitation and emission wavelength in heterostructure. In the E-(CDs@F) heterostructure, the excitation light is coupled to a leaky mode, leading to enhancement of excitation, while in the CDs@F layer, the fluorescence light is coupled to a leaky mode that leads to enhanced extraction [29,30], resulting in an enhancement factor around 56-fold.

The E-(CDs@F)-E double heterostructure produced the highest fluorescence enhancement factor of 73-fold. The additional enhancement in the E-(CDs@F)-E double heterostructure arises because the two E layers form an optical resonator for the excitation light, enhancing the excitation intensity and thus the fluorescence intensity. In other words, the multiple beam interference caused by the top and bottom E layers as illustrated in Schematic 1 has given the extra enhancement effect; The excitation light bounces back and forth in the middle CDs@F layer in E-(CDs@F)-E, and this photon recycling significantly enhances the excitation, as if the light path has been extended many times within the F layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg(II) ion detection sensitivity and selectivity
ce:section.ce:para:
We further investigated the detection sensitivity of the double heterostructure E-(CDs@F)-E film towards Hg(II). The sensitivity to Hg(II) arises from the NH2 ligands on CDs surface, as described in detail in [9]; The coordination of Hg(II) with the NH2 terminals on CDs causes electron transfer, which exhibits FL quenching. A series of Hg(II) aqueous solution with different concentrations varied from 20 pM to 2000 pM and the blank water as a control sample were used to interact with E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor. The results demonstrated that there were significant decreases of the FL intensity for Hg(II) solution compared to control sample. Fig. 3 (a) shows the FL spectra of the E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor decreased with increasing the concentration of Hg(II) ion. Inset figures exhibit the FL quenching efficiency which can be fitted by the Stern–Volmer equation:
(2)
[
F 0
F
]
− 1 =
K
S V
C
Where F0 and F are the FL intensity of E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor in the absence and presence of Hg(II) ion; KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and C is the concentration of Hg(II) ion. The detection limit, DL, [31] was determined to be 19 pM using the equation
D L =
3 σ
m
, where σ is the relative standard deviation, and m the slope of calibration curve as described in detail in Fig. S2. We obtained good linear correlation of R2
=0.9899 over the range of 100–750 pM for the detection of Hg(II). By comparison of detection limit between solution based CDs [9] and E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor in Fig. S3, we observed that the DL is improved by 13-fold from 1.3nM to 91pM for Hg(II). The E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor showed superior sensing sensitivity for the detection of trace Hg(II) compared to similar solid state sensors based on monolithic CPhCs [20,22,32,33].

For the application of present system to monitor the water quality, the concentrations of Hg(II) ion was determined in the secondary wastewater sample collected from Redland wastewater plant, Queensland, Australia. As shown in Fig. 3(b), The fluorescence quenching of E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor by different concentration of Hg(II) in the wastewater sample were investigated. Due to the complex nature of wastewater and its auto-fluorescence, there is a small degree of sensitivity loss of the E-(CDs@F)-E film, however, still extraordinary high compared to other solid-state sensors; The Stern–Volmer plot shows the linear range of 0–200nM with R2
=0.9041. The detection limit for Hg(II) in wastewater was calculated to be 2nM. The detection limit of film sensor (2nM) enhanced 24-fold compare to solution based sensor in our previous study (50nM).

To investigate whether the immobilized CDs in E-(CDs@F)-E film also retains their sensing selectivity, FL quenching efficiencies of the E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor were examined by testing the response to various ions, including Na+, Ag+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2− and SO4 2−. The FL quenching efficiencies obtained from different ions are shown in Fig. 3(c) and Fig. S4. Under the same mild reaction conditions, E-(CDs@F)-E film sensor has only a slight FL quenching in the presence of these ions, even at a concentration of 100μM of metal ions in Fig. 3(c). The E-(CDs@F)-E film retains the CDs’ high sensitivity to Fe(III) ions [9], as demonstrated in Fig. 3(c). The detection limit of Fe(III) was determined to be 19 pM linear correlation of R2
=0.97 over the range of 20–100pM (Fig. S5 and S6). However, it is interesting to observe that for E-(CDs@F)-E film, the application of Fe(III) masking agent, namely sodium hexametaphosphate (SHPP), did not result in full masking effect as shown when CDs are free in solution [9]. This suggest that the binding of CDs with Hg(II) is slightly weaker after CDs immobilization on PS surface. Overall, these results confirmed that the CDs incorporated in E-(CDs@F)-E film by ‘under the skin’ coating method have retained their selectivity to Hg(II), with enhanced sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility of the double heterostructure film sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Since now the sensing agents, CDs, are immobilized on the double heterostructure CPhCs, it is possible to apply washing steps for making them into reusable sensors.

Usually high sensitivity chemosensor implies high affinity between the sensing agents and the analytes, the attachment may not be reversible. Here, we apply cysteine as the washing solution for ‘scrubbing’ off Hg(II) ions, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a).

To test the reversibility, the E-(CD@F)-E film sensor which had been reacted with Hg(II) ions, was immersed in the aqueous solution of 1.0mM cysteine for 15min at room temperature, followed by rinsing with deionised water and drying in the room temperature. The terminal amino groups with trapped Hg(II) could be recovered to the original state because the binding between cysteine and the Hg(II) ions is stronger than that between CDs and Hg(II). Therefore, the NH2–Hg(II)–NH2 complexes were destroyed and the Hg(II) ions were removed, which resulted in the recovery of the detecting function. Fig. 4(b) shows the FL intensities of the E-(CD@F)-E film sensor when immersed alternately in the solutions of 10nM Hg(II) ions and 1.0mM cysteine for 6 regeneration cycles. It is clearly seen that the FL intensity changed reversibly by washing with cysteine solution. Significant sensitivity, selectivity, reusability and more importantly strong stability of E-(CD@F)-E film sensor in this study may lead to potential strategy for developing the simple and highly sensitive solid based metal ions sensor.

We have successfully developed an effective fluorescence-based E-(CD@F)-E double heterostructure film sensor for Hg(II) sensing in water samples. The original CDs fluorescence emission at 460nm was amplified 73-fold in the double heterostructure CPhC by the coupling photon recycling and the photonic modes, exhibiting superior signal-to-noise ratio compared to monolithic or heterostructure CPhC-based sensing enhancement platforms. When used for Hg(II) sensing, the limit of detection has reached 91pM, 13 fold lower than CDs in suspension. We have also demonstrated that the diffusion and entrapment method utilising polymer swelling and de-swelling properties in different solvents is also effective for incorporating nanoparticles in the skin layer of the polymer beads. This ‘under the skin’ coating method does not require formation of chemical bonds, yet stable and robust, which serves as an excellent coating method for immobilizing sensing agents on a solid-state matrix used in liquid phase.

In addition, the film sensor can be easily regenerated by decoupling NH2–Hg(II)–NH2 complex using cysteine solution. Therefore, the obtained E-(CD@F)-E double heterostructure film sensor for detecting Hg(II) ions is highly sensitive, selective and reusable which shows a promising future for sensing technology. The strategy developed here is also applicable to systems for detection of other elements using different fluorescent based probes immobilised in double heterostructure film sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the Australia Research Council (ARC) Centre under the Project DP160104089, Wentai Wang acknowledges the support of China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M590660), and the access to the electron microscopes at the Centre for Microscopy and Microanalysis at the University of Queensland. We also thank Mr. Bradley Taylor at Redland City Council for providing wastewater samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.08.154.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for detection of lead, cadmium, and mercury ions
Chem. Soc. Rev.
Gold nanospikes based microsensor as a highly accurate mercury emission monitoring system
Sci. Rep.
Enhanced sensitivity of a direct SERS technique for Hg2+ detection based on the investigation of the interaction between silver nanoparticles and mercury ions
Nanoscale
Plasmon enhanced photoelectrochemical sensing of mercury (II) ions in human serum based on Au@Ag nanorods modified TiO2 nanosheets film
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ehsan Eftekhari received his Ph.D. degree from Griffith University. Now he is a postdoctoral research fellow at the Queensland Micro and Nanotechnology Centre and Environmental Engineering, Griffith University. His main scientific interests are the design and synthesis of enabling platforms with advanced materials and structural diversity for various applications.

Wentai Wang received his Ph.D. degree from Griffith University. Currently, he is an associate professor at Ocean University of China. His main scientific interests are the design and synthesis of smart materials for various applications.

Xiang Li received his B.S. in Materials Chemistry (2010) from Peking University, and M.E in Environmental Engineering (2014) from Griffith University. He currently is a Mphil student under at Griffith University. His research is focused on the nanoparticle synthesis and environmental sensing technologies.

Nikhil A recieved his M.Tech degree in Nanotechnology from Amrita vishwa vidyapeetham Univeristy in 2015. Currently, he is a PhD student at Griffith university. His main research interest lies on how light matter interaction can be manipulated/controlled at nanoscale level relevant to the field of photovoltaics, photocatalysis and sensors.

Zhiqing Wu received her M.E. degree in Environmental Engineering from Griffith University in 2016. Currently, she is a MPhil student under the supervision of Prof. Qin Li. Her research interest includes the preparation of smart materials and their sensing and photocatalysis applications.

Robin Klein received his B.E. degree in Environmental Engineering from Griffith University in 2016. Currently, he is a graduate student at Griffith University. His major research arrears focus on synthesis of nanoparticles.

Ivan S. Cole is a Director of High Performance Metal Industries at CSIRO, Australia. He combines broad experience in the areas of research management and planning with a detailed knowledge of materials science and mathematical modelling.

Qin Li is an Associate Professor at the Queensland Micro- and Nanotechnology Centre and Environmental Engineering, Griffith University. She has established a dynamic and enthused team in functional nanomaterials and nanotechnology for environment and energy with a particular focus on nanocarbons and photonic crystals.
